original_abstract: Since taking office in 2010, the Hungarian government, led by the populist radical right Fidesz party, has gained international notoriety for restructuring domestic institutions to serve an illiberal political agenda. This systematic democratic backsliding has placed Hungary on a collision course with the European Union, culminating in the European Commission triggering its new rule-of-law conditionality mechanism in April 2022. By applying a model of cross-sectoral bargaining, this article argues that the EU’s standoff with Hungary over democratic standards has become inextricably linked to foreign policy, specifically regarding the EU’s aid packages to Ukraine. While decision-making within the Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) has traditionally been viewed as an "insulated" process, our findings reveal an increasing instrumentalization of foreign policy to extract concessions in internal EU disputes. Ultimately, this case illustrates the broader "domestication" of foreign policy by populist actors, where international security matters are leveraged as bargaining chips in domestic and intra-institutional power struggles.
humanized_abstract: After taking power in 2010, Hungary’s centre right government led by the populist and radical right Fidesz party has made headlines around the world due to reforms to the constitution and wider institutions aimed at the promotion of an illiberal style of democracy, making it at odds with a large part of the European Union (EU). Using an innovative theory of cross-sectoral bargaining, the article shows that, as of 2022, tensions in the EU surrounding the democratic criteria to be met by member-states have become ever more closely intertwined with the EU’s Common Foreign and Security Policy — specifically with the European aid programme aimed at Ukraine. Contrary to the established image of EU foreign policy decision-making as ‘insulated’, the article provides substantial evidence suggesting the ever-increasing instrumentalisation by EU member-states of foreign policy and thereby of international issues for bargaining and strategic use in relation to purely domestic EU matters, including the struggles for power that constantly erupt at the domestic level. More generally, the article highlights a new trend known as the ‘domestication of foreign policy’ as a whole, which has seen the use of issues related to security on the world scale increasingly for domestic and, more specifically, for intra-institutional purposes.
